summary: This source outlines the core philosophical ideas of Socrates, emphasizing the necessity of self-examination and the care of the soul. It details his beliefs that knowledge of virtue leads to happiness, that all evil is committed involuntarily out of ignorance, and that suffering an injustice is always preferable to committing one.

principles.statement: The unexamined life is not worth living.
principles.rationale: Without turning our gaze inward to analyze our true nature and values, we cannot attain the self-knowledge necessary to answer how we ought to live and improve our lives.
principles.source_quote: the unexamined life is not worth living.

principles.statement: Care for the perfection of your soul above wealth, reputation, and honor.
principles.rationale: The true self is the soul, not the body or social status. The state of our inner being determines the quality of our life, making it paramount to devote our energy to making the soul as good and beautiful as possible.
principles.source_quote: Most excellent man, are you…not ashamed to care for the acquisition of wealth and for reputation and honor, when you neither care nor take thought for wisdom and truth and the perfection of your soul?

principles.statement: All evil acts are committed involuntarily out of ignorance.
principles.rationale: Human beings naturally desire happiness and the good. When people commit evil, it is because they falsely estimate what is good (e.g., mistaking wealth or power for true good) and are ignorant of the fact that their actions tarnish their soul.
principles.source_quote: My own opinion is more or less this: no wise man believes that anyone sins willingly or willingly perpetuates any base or evil act; they know very well that every base or evil action is committed involuntarily.

principles.statement: It is far worse to commit an injustice than to suffer one.
principles.rationale: Committing an injustice harms your own soul, which is your true self and the source of your happiness. Suffering an injustice only harms your possessions, reputation, or body.
principles.source_quote: So I spoke the truth when I said that neither I nor you nor any other man would rather do injustice than suffer it: for it is worse.

frameworks.name: The Path to Virtue and Happiness
frameworks.when_to_apply: When seeking to live a good life and attain true happiness.
steps: Turn your gaze inward to attain self-knowledge and discover your true nature.
Recognize that your true self is your soul, not your possessions, status, or body.
Obtain knowledge of what is truly good (virtue) and what is truly evil.
Use this knowledge to cultivate the good within your soul and purge the evil from it.
frameworks.source_quote: …once we know ourselves, we may learn how to care for ourselves, but otherwise we never shall.

mental_models.name: Evil as Ignorance
mental_models.description: A lens for understanding human misbehavior: no one intentionally chooses to be evil or unhappy. People do bad things because they have a mistaken conception of what will bring them happiness, falsely expecting to gain good (like wealth or power) while failing to realize the immeasurable damage they are doing to their own soul.
mental_models.example: An individual who uses evil means to attain power, falsely assuming power is the greatest good, while remaining ignorant that they are condemning themselves to perpetual unhappiness by tarnishing their soul.

mental_models.name: The Soul as the True Self
mental_models.description: The perspective that a person's identity and ultimate worth are located entirely in their inner being (the soul), rather than in external metrics like physical health, social status, or material wealth.
mental_models.example: A virtuous individual treating the danger of life or death as a trivial matter, focusing solely on whether their actions are just or unjust.

heuristics: When acting, consider only whether the action is just or unjust, not the danger of life or death.

signature_quotes.text: the unexamined life is not worth living.
signature_quotes.context: From Plato's Apology, regarding the importance of self-knowledge.

signature_quotes.text: …once we know ourselves, we may learn how to care for ourselves, but otherwise we never shall.
signature_quotes.context: From First Alcibiades, on the prerequisite of self-knowledge for self-improvement.

signature_quotes.text: Man, you don’t speak well, if you believe that a man worth anything at all would give countervailing weight to the danger of life or death, or give consideration to anything but this when he acts: whether his action is just or unjust, the action of a good or of an evil man.
signature_quotes.context: From Plato's Apology, on prioritizing virtue over physical survival.

signature_quotes.text: So I spoke the truth when I said that neither I nor you nor any other man would rather do injustice than suffer it: for it is worse.
signature_quotes.context: From Gorgias, asserting that harming one's own soul through injustice is the greatest evil.

anti_patterns.description: Unquestioningly adhering to cultural values and norms to determine the course of one's life.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It prevents individuals from consciously contemplating how they ought to live, blocking the self-knowledge required to actually improve their lives.

anti_patterns.description: Regarding wealth, status, pleasure, and social acceptance as the greatest goods in life.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: These are false goods. Chasing them leads to a mistaken conception of happiness, causing people to spend their lives frantically pursuing things that will not make them happy, often committing evil acts that damage their souls in the process.
